title: Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Disease burden from non-communicable diseases vs. GDP per capita, 2023

Disease burden to non-communicable diseases (NCDs), measured in DALYs (Disability-Adjusted Life Years) per 100,000 individuals versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and health outcomes, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against the disease burden from non-communicable diseases on the vertical axis. The data points, sized by population, show a general negative correlation where countries with higher GDP per capita typically record lower DALYs per 100,000 people. At the lower end of the economic scale, nations in Africa and Oceania display high disease burdens; Nauru sits at the top of the chart with over 50,000 DALYs, while the Solomon Islands, Marshall Islands, and Myanmar cluster between 30,000 and 40,000 DALYs despite having GDPs under $10,000. In the middle range, countries like India, Indonesia, and China show burdens between 15,000 and 25,000 DALYs with GDPs ranging from roughly $5,000 to $20,000. As GDP rises above $20,000, the data points shift downward, with the United States, Germany, and Poland all falling below 25,000 DALYs. Singapore appears at the far right with a GDP approaching $100,000 and a disease burden near 15,000 DALYs. The color coding distinguishes regions, with African nations (purple) concentrated in the upper-left quadrant and European nations (blue) clustered in the lower-right. The chart notes that GDP per capita is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices. The visible pattern shows that higher economic output per person coincides with a lower burden of non-communicable diseases across the global dataset.
